Emphasis: the extreme read fan-out of a single hot match (CDN + edge + fan-out tree / pub-sub), push vs poll to clients, the tiny-write-huge-read asymmetry, hot-match as the binding bottleneck.

Report PASS/FAIL with evidence on each:
1. ALL 11 sections present and fully expanded; md > 20,000 chars. §1 must OPEN with the 🔒 scope-lock (forking assumptions FIRST), then derived functional requirements, then provisional NFRs. §2 must be the residual forks and must NOT re-ask anything §1 already locked (flag any duplication).
2. CAPACITY (§3): numbers actually derived + self-consistent (re-run capacity.py with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python if present); a named decision-forcing number + flip threshold; a peak-from-average burst factor; no false precision.
3. TECHNICAL CORRECTNESS for THIS problem + the emphasis: are the deep dives the genuinely hardest parts (binding bottleneck, hot-key, global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, back-pressure)? Find any wrong or hand-wavy claim.
4. MERMAID SAFETY (CRITICAL — many diagrams): scan EVERY ```mermaid block; FAIL any label/edge-text containing breaking chars — parentheses, a colon, a semicolon, slashes, quotes, angle brackets, curly braces. Report the exact offending line. Confirm flowchart TD for multi-path.
5. §5 has real REST endpoints + JSON + idempotency + object-level authz + versioning/pagination noted. §7 has 5-part storage cards + PART D data lifecycle. §9 covers back-pressure, observability, rate-limiting. §10 has CAP/PACELC line.
6. Markdown hygiene: no LaTeX, no stray tags, tables well-formed, emoji-script structure present.

content.content:
1	# Design a Live Cricket Score & Analytics Platform (Cricbuzz)
2	
3	The core tension in one line: the **write side is trivial** — a few events per over, maybe one update every fifteen seconds — but the **read side is one of the most extreme fan-outs in consumer software**: a single India-Pakistan World Cup final can put **tens of millions of people on the same score page at the same instant**, all wanting the next ball *now*. So this is not a database problem. It's a **distribution problem**: how do you take one tiny write and deliver it to 50 million screens in under a second, cheaply.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions
8	
9	> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one flow at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — which here are all about read fan-out. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"
10	
11	> 💬 "Before I design anything, let me lock the few decisions that actually fork the architecture — because for a live-scores product, the answer to 'push or poll' and 'how hot is the hottest match' literally decides the whole shape of the system."
12	
13	### 🔒 Scope lock (vetoable assumptions — stop me if any is wrong)
14	
15	1. **Reads dominate writes by ~10⁷:1, and ONE hot match dominates all others.** I'll assume a single marquee match (a World Cup final) drives ~50M concurrent viewers, while writes are ~1 event every 15s. *This is the binding constraint — everything below is built around it.* If instead load is spread evenly across thousands of small matches, the hot-match machinery relaxes into ordinary sharding.
16	2. **Clients want near-real-time updates (~1–2s), so we PUSH, not poll.** I'll assume we own the client apps and can hold a persistent connection (WebSocket or SSE). If we *must* support dumb polling clients (old browsers, third-party embeds), we keep a CDN-cached poll fallback — but push is the primary path.
17	3. **The score is eventually consistent and read-mostly; there is no money path.** A viewer seeing a ball 1–2 seconds late is fine; two viewers seeing slightly different counts for a few hundred milliseconds is fine. There is exactly **one authoritative writer per match** (the scorer/feed), so we sidestep write conflicts entirely.
18	4. **Global audience, single authoritative write region.** I'll assume writes for a match land in one region (wherever the feed originates) and fan out globally to read replicas/edges. Multi-master writing would be pointless here — there's one source of truth per match.
19	
20	> 🤝 **In/out of scope — add or cut before I commit?** In scope: live score, ball-by-ball commentary, live stats/leaderboards, push delivery, the analytics scorecard. Out of scope (below the line): video streaming, betting/odds, user accounts & social, fantasy leagues, ML predictions. *Those are separate systems — I'll note where they'd hang off this one.*
21	
22	**Since reads dominate one hot match and we're pushing near-real-time updates, the core flows are:**
23	
24	### Functional requirements (above the line)
25	
26	**F1 — Ingest a live event (the tiny write).** The official scorer/feed posts each ball: runs, wickets, extras, plus a commentary line.
27	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Someone courtside taps 'four runs, ball 34.2' and it becomes the new truth for that match."
28	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** it's NOT hard on throughput — it's hard because this one write must trigger a fan-out to 50M screens, and it must be *ordered and idempotent* (the scorer's app retries on a flaky stadium network).
29	
30	**F2 — Stream the live score & commentary to viewers (the huge read).** Every viewer's screen updates within ~1–2s of each ball.
31	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "50 million people are staring at the same number, and when a wicket falls everyone's screen flips at once."
32	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is the whole game. Naively each viewer polling is ~5 million requests/second hitting origin — impossible. We must absorb it at the edge and push deltas, not poll full pages.
33	
34	**F3 — Serve live stats & leaderboards (derived reads).** Current run rate, partnership, top scorers across matches, "most sixes today."
35	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The little widgets — run rate, who's batting well, the day's leaderboard — update live too."
36	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** these are *aggregations* recomputed on every event; the cross-match leaderboard is a hot, frequently-read derived value that must not require scanning every match on every read.
37	
38	**F4 — Serve the full scorecard & post-match analytics (cold-ish reads).** The detailed ball-by-ball card, wagon wheels, manhattan charts — live during the match, archived forever after.
39	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The detailed scorecard you scroll through, and the same thing six months later as history."
40	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** it's a *different access pattern* — heavier payload, deep history, OLAP-style queries — so it can't share the hot-path store with the tiny live blob.
41	
42	**Below the line (out of scope):** video/audio streaming, betting odds, push *notifications* to logged-out devices, fantasy scoring, user social graph, payments. *(Video is 1000x the bandwidth and a separate CDN problem; I'll mention the seam.)*
43	
44	### Non-functional requirements (PROVISIONAL targets, tagged to assumptions)
45	
46	**NFR1 — Freshness: a ball reaches every screen in p99 < 2s** (assuming push + assumption 2).
47	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Worst case, your screen is two seconds behind the stadium — usually under one."
48	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** people open Twitter/TV to find out the score is wrong on our app; we lose the one thing we exist for. A live-scores app that's 30s late is dead.
49	
50	**NFR2 — Read availability: 4 nines (99.99%) on the read path** (assuming reads are the product).
51	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Down less than ~1 hour per *year* total — and ideally never during a final."
52	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if we're down for the last over of a World Cup final, that's a brand-ending event; this is a "the whole country is watching" workload.
53	
54	**NFR3 — Hot-match scale: survive 50M concurrent viewers on ONE match with graceful degradation** (assumption 1).
55	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "One match can be the entire load; we must not fall over when 50M people pile onto it."
56	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the hot match takes down every other match too — a single shard meltdown becomes a total outage.
57	
58	**NFR4 — Cost-efficient at peak: egress is the bill, so push deltas not full pages** (assumption 2).
59	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We pay by the byte sent; sending tiny diffs instead of whole pages is the difference between a sane bill and a ruinous one."
60	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a single big final could cost more in CDN egress than a month of normal operation; cost is a first-class design input here, not an afterthought.
61	
62	**NFR5 — Ordering & idempotency on the write: events apply in order, exactly once** (assumption 3).
63	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Ball 34.2 never shows up before 34.1, and a retried submission doesn't double-count a six."
64	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the score is wrong — a wicket gets duplicated or a ball goes missing — which is unforgivable for a scores product.
65	
66	> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** the read-your-own-writes invariant is *inverted* here — there's one writer (the scorer) and millions of read-only viewers, so the classic RYOW race barely applies; I'll instead make sure the *scorer* always sees their own last submission immediately (read-from-primary), and note that viewer-to-viewer consistency is intentionally eventual within ~1–2s.
67	
68	> 🎙️ **Script:** "So the requirements split cleanly. The write side — ingesting each ball — is tiny and I'll barely spend time on it, except to make it ordered and idempotent. The read side is the entire system: getting one ball out to fifty million screens in under two seconds, reliably, without going bankrupt on bandwidth. My targets are: under two seconds to every screen, four nines of read availability, survive a single fifty-million-viewer match, and keep egress cheap by pushing diffs not pages. The honest gap I'd flag is that read-your-own-writes is almost a non-issue here because there's one writer and a sea of readers."
69	
70	---
71	
72	## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)
73	
74	> These are the residual forks from my scope lock — the places my design is most sensitive — so you can redirect me before I over-invest.
75	
76	**Fork A — I assumed ONE hot match dominates (50M concurrent on a single match).**
77	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
78	  - If load is actually **spread evenly** across thousands of small matches → I drop the per-match hot-shard machinery and the celebrity-match fan-out tree; ordinary consistent-hash sharding by match_id handles it, and this becomes a much more boring design.
79	  - If it's **even more concentrated / unpredictable** (a 100M-viewer event) → I lean harder into multi-tier fan-out and pre-warming the edge before the match starts.
80	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "If your real traffic is lots of small matches rather than one giant final, tell me — I'll drop the hot-match tree and just shard by match, because that's a much simpler system."
81	
82	**Fork B — I assumed we PUSH over a persistent connection (we own the client).**
83	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
84	  - If we **can't hold persistent connections** (must support dumb HTTP clients, third-party embeds) → the design pivots to **CDN-cached short-TTL polling** as the primary path; the fan-out tree becomes a fallback, and freshness loosens from ~1s to roughly the TTL (~3–5s).
85	  - If we **fully own modern clients** → push is clearly better: cheaper bytes, lower latency, no thundering-herd on TTL expiry.
86	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "My primary path is push over WebSocket. If you need to support arbitrary browsers or embeds that can only poll, I'll flip to CDN-cached polling — it costs a couple seconds of freshness and more bytes, but it's bulletproof and dumb."
87	
88	**Fork C — I assumed eventual consistency with one authoritative writer (no money path).**
89	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
90	  - If there were a **money path** (live betting settling on our score) → the score becomes a strong-consistency, audited, single-source-of-truth value with signed events; freshness and correctness SLAs tighten enormously.
91	  - If multiple writers (multiple scorers cross-checking) → I'd need a conflict-resolution / consensus story on the write, which I currently get to skip entirely.
92	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm treating the score as read-mostly and eventually consistent because nobody's betting on it through us. If money rides on this number, stop me — that's a different, much stricter design."
93	
94	> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are the three forks. If you're happy with one-hot-match, push-primary, eventually-consistent, I'll go compute the scale numbers — because the read fan-out number is what justifies every later decision. Want to redirect any of these first?"
95	
96	---
97	
98	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
99	
100	I computed these in a small script (`capacity.py`) so I can defend the arithmetic, not hand-wave it. *(QPS = queries per second, how many requests hit a tier each second. Concurrent = people connected at the exact same instant. TTL = time-to-live, how long a cached value is served before it's refetched.)*
101	
102	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
103	|---|---|---|---|---|
104	| **Write rate (1 match)** | 300 balls × 2 innings × ~2 events/ball = 1,200 events ÷ (8h × 3600s) | **~0.04 events/s** | "About one event every fifteen seconds" | Writes are free — spend zero design budget on write throughput |
105	| **Global write QPS** | ~0.04/s × 20 concurrent matches | **~1 QPS** | "Maybe one write a second across the whole planet" | A single small primary handles ALL writes; no write sharding for throughput |
106	| **Naive poll read QPS** | 50M viewers ÷ 10s poll interval | **~5,000,000 QPS** | "Five million requests a second if everyone polls" | **THE decision number** — origin cannot serve this; must absorb at edge |
107	| **Last-over poll QPS** | 50M ÷ 2s (impatient polling) | **~25,000,000 QPS** | "Twenty-five million a second in the last over" | Worst-case burst; pre-warm edge + push to avoid it entirely |
108	| **Read:write ratio** | 5M reads/s ÷ 0.04 writes/s | **~10⁸ : 1** | "A hundred million reads per write" | Asymmetry IS the system — design entirely around read fan-out |
109	| **Origin QPS behind CDN** | 250 PoPs ÷ 5s TTL (one miss per edge per TTL) | **~50 QPS** | "Fifty requests a second at origin" | CDN collapses 5M→50, a ~100,000:1 collapse — origin becomes trivial |
110	| **Persistent connections** | 50M concurrent viewers (push) | **~50M conns** | "Fifty million open sockets" | Need a fan-out tier; one box ≈ 100K conns |
111	| **Fan-out boxes** | 50M conns ÷ 100K conns/box | **~500 boxes** | "About five hundred edge boxes" | This is the unit we scale; sharded by match |
112	| **Push egress (peak)** | 50M × 480 deltas/hr × 300 B | **~7,200 GB/hr** | "Seven terabytes an hour" | vs poll's ~36 TB/hr → push is **~5x cheaper bytes** |
113	| **Egress cost (peak month)** | 7,200 GB/hr × 40 peak-hr × $0.05/GB | **~$14K/mo** at the marquee peak; egress dominates total | "Egress is the bill, DB is a rounding error" | Spend on edge/egress optimization, not DB |
114	| **Unit economics** | egress cost ÷ deliveries | **~$0.015 / 1M deliveries** | "About a penty-and-a-half per million pushes" | Push + deltas is the cost-efficient unit |
115	| **Storage (5yr, all balls)** | 5,000 matches/yr × 1,200 events × 1KB × 5yr × 3 repl × 2 idx | **~0.2 TB** | "A couple hundred gigs" | Storage is a non-issue — ignore it |
116	
117	> **The ONE decision-forcing number:** **~5 million read requests/second** at origin if everyone polls naively. Origin physically can't serve it. **Flip threshold:** the moment a single fan-out box exceeds ~100K connections, or a single origin partition exceeds its QPS ceiling, we **add a fan-out tier / split the hot match across more edge boxes**. That single number — 5M/s collapsing to ~50/s at origin — justifies the CDN + push fan-out tree that the rest of the design is built on.
118	
119	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The numbers tell one story. Writes are about one event every fifteen seconds — genuinely a rounding error, I'll spend no time scaling them. Reads are the opposite: if fifty million people poll, that's five million requests a second hitting origin, which is impossible. So the whole job is collapsing that five million down — a CDN plus push fan-out takes origin from five million a second to about fifty. Storage is a couple hundred gigs, a non-issue. The bill is bandwidth, and pushing tiny diffs instead of whole pages cuts that roughly fivefold. So I'll spend my entire design budget on read fan-out and egress, not on the database."
120	
121	---
122	
123	## 4. Core Entities
124	
125	This is a first-draft scaffold — I'll add full fields in the data model (§7).
126	
127	- **Match** — the live game itself; owns its current state (teams, innings, status: live/over) and is the unit we shard and fan out by.
128	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** "Think of a Match like a live TV channel — it has one broadcast everyone tunes into, and the hot one is the channel everybody's on at once."
129	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you handle the one match that's 1000x hotter than the rest?" → "We shard the fan-out per match, and the hot match gets its own dedicated set of edge boxes so it can't starve the others."
130	
131	- **ScoreEvent** — one immutable ball (or wicket/extra): the atomic write, ordered within a match.
132	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** "Like a single entry in a logbook — you only ever append, never edit, so the log of balls is the source of truth."
133	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you guarantee order and no duplicates?" → "Each event carries a monotonic sequence number per match; the writer is single, and we dedup on (match_id, seq)."
134	
135	- **ScoreSnapshot** — the current materialized score blob for a match (latest state, last-6-balls), the thing actually served on the hot path.
136	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** "The scoreboard on the wall — it's just the *current* picture, recomputed each ball, so readers never replay the whole log."
137	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Why a snapshot and not replaying events?" → "Replaying 1,200 events on every one of 5M reads is insane; we materialize once per ball and serve the tiny blob."
138	
139	- **Commentary** — the human/auto text line attached to each ball.
140	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** "Like the radio commentator's sentence for that ball — it rides along with the score event."
141	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Same store as the score?" → "It's part of the event but can lag the bare score; we push score first, commentary can trail by a beat."
142	
143	- **Leaderboard / StatAggregate** — derived cross-match rankings (top scorers today, most sixes), recomputed on events.
144	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** "Like a running tally board updated as results come in — nobody recomputes it from scratch on every glance."
145	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "How is the cross-match leaderboard not a giant scan?" → "It's an incrementally-updated sorted structure (a Redis sorted set), updated on each event, read in O(log n)."
146	
147	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are simple. There's the Match — the channel everyone tunes into and the thing I shard by. There are immutable ScoreEvents — append-only balls, the source of truth. The one to call out is the ScoreSnapshot: I materialize the current score once per ball into a tiny blob, because replaying twelve hundred events on every one of five million reads would be madness. Then there's Commentary that rides along, and derived Leaderboards I keep in a sorted structure so reads stay cheap. I'll flesh out the fields and stores in the data model."
148	
149	---
150	
151	## 5. API / Interface
152	
153	All endpoints are versioned under `/v1`. Lists use **cursor pagination** (a `cursor` token, not offset/limit — offset gets slower and skips/duplicates rows as new balls land under it). **Security:** the *scorer* is identified by JWT (a signed token proving who's calling) with a per-match scorer role; viewers are unauthenticated or lightly-authed. **Object-level authz:** the write endpoint checks "is THIS caller the assigned scorer for THIS match?" — not just "is this a valid user?"
154	
155	### F1 — Ingest an event (write)
156	- **Method+path:** `POST /v1/matches/{matchId}/events` — POST because it appends new immutable state.
157	- **Key fields:**
158	  - `seq: int` — monotonic per-match sequence; the writer assigns it so we can detect gaps/dups. *Why:* idempotency + ordering.
159	  - `type: string` — `ball | wicket | extra | milestone`. *Why:* tells consumers how to update derived stats.
160	  - `payload: object` — runs, batsman, bowler, commentary text.
161	  - `idempotencyKey: string` — survives the scorer's flaky-network retry.
162	  - **Absent on purpose:** the server sets `appliedAt` and the authoritative `globalOffset`; the client never sets timestamps or global ordering — we don't trust a stadium clock.
163	```
164	// Request
165	POST /v1/matches/m_ind_pak_2026/events
166	Idempotency-Key: scorer_a1b2
167	{ "seq": 342, "type": "ball", "payload": { "runs": 4, "batsman": "p_kohli", "bowler": "p_afridi", "commentary": "Driven through covers for four" } }
168	
169	// Response 202
170	{ "matchId": "m_ind_pak_2026", "seq": 342, "status": "APPLIED", "appliedAt": "2026-06-21T14:32:08Z" }
171	```
172	- **Key decision:** **idempotency on `(matchId, idempotencyKey)`** with a short-TTL dedup store. A retried submission returns the same `APPLIED` result, never double-counts. Returns `202 Accepted` — applied to the log, fan-out is async.
173	
174	### F2 — Subscribe to the live stream (the huge read)
175	- **Method+path:** `GET /v1/matches/{matchId}/stream` — a GET that **upgrades to WebSocket/SSE** (a long-lived connection the server pushes down). GET because it's a pure read subscription.
176	- **Key fields (query):** `sinceSeq: int` — the last ball the client already has, so on reconnect we replay only the gap (catch-up), not the whole match.
177	- **What flows down:** small **deltas** (just the changed ball), not the full page.
178	```
179	// Client opens
180	GET /v1/matches/m_ind_pak_2026/stream?sinceSeq=341
181	Upgrade: websocket
182	
183	// Server pushes (one frame per ball)
184	{ "seq": 342, "type": "ball", "score": "287/4", "lastBall": { "runs": 4 }, "commentary": "Driven through covers for four" }
185	```
186	- **Key decision:** **push deltas with a `sinceSeq` resume token.** On reconnect (mobile network blips constantly), the client says "I have up to 341," and we replay 342+ from a small ring buffer — no full refetch, no missed ball.
187	
188	### F3 — Fallback poll + leaderboards
189	- **Method+path:** `GET /v1/matches/{matchId}/score` — CDN-cached current snapshot for dumb/poll clients.
190	- `GET /v1/leaderboards/{boardId}?cursor=...` — cross-match rankings, cursor-paginated.
191	```
192	// Request
193	GET /v1/matches/m_ind_pak_2026/score
194	
195	// Response 200 (Cache-Control: max-age=3, stale-while-revalidate=10)
196	{ "matchId": "m_ind_pak_2026", "score": "287/4", "overs": "57.0", "lastSeq": 342 }
197	```
198	- **Key decision:** the poll response is **CDN-cacheable with a ~3–5s TTL** plus `stale-while-revalidate` so a TTL expiry never stampedes origin — at most one edge fetches while everyone else serves slightly stale.
199	
200	### F4 — Full scorecard / analytics
201	- **Method+path:** `GET /v1/matches/{matchId}/scorecard?cursor=...` — heavier, deep history, served from a different store; cacheable longer once a match ends.
202	
203	> 💬 "Notice the write returns 202 and the heavy lifting is the stream endpoint — the API shape itself tells you reads are the product."
204	
205	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The write API is one endpoint — POST an event with a sequence number and an idempotency key, so a retry on a flaky stadium connection never double-counts. The star is the stream endpoint: it's a GET that upgrades to a WebSocket and pushes tiny deltas — just the changed ball. The clever bit is the `sinceSeq` resume token: mobile connections drop constantly, so on reconnect the client says 'I have up to ball 341' and we replay only the gap. For clients that can't hold a socket, there's a CDN-cached poll endpoint with a few seconds of TTL and stale-while-revalidate so a cache expiry never stampedes origin."

209	## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time)
210	
211	Shared color language across all diagrams: blue = clients, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores/caches, purple = external.
212	
213	### 6.1 — F1: Ingest a live event (the tiny write)
214	
215	This slice is small but load-bearing: one ordered, idempotent write that becomes the trigger for everything downstream.
216	
217	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off accepted |
218	|---|---|---|---|---|
219	| Ingest service | A tiny stateless API that validates the scorer's event and appends it | At ~1 write/s globally, this is trivial — its only job is auth, idempotency, ordering | Not a heavy write cluster — we have ~1 write/s, that'd be absurd | Single small primary is fine; not built for write scale |
220	| Event log | An append-only ordered log of events per match (Kafka-style) | We need ordered, replayable, durable events that the fan-out and analytics both consume | Not direct DB writes only — we'd lose the replay/fan-out decoupling | One more moving piece, but it's the backbone |
221	| Snapshot store | An in-memory store holding the current materialized score blob | Readers must NOT replay 1,200 events — materialize once per ball | Not recompute-on-read — that's 5M replays/s, insane | Snapshot can lag the log by milliseconds |
222	
223	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The ingest service is deliberately tiny — at one write a second, all it does is check the JWT, dedup on the idempotency key, and assign ordering. It appends to an event log, which is just a durable, ordered, replayable stream of balls — I picked a log because both the fan-out tier and the analytics pipeline want to consume the same ordered events independently. Then a small worker materializes the current score into a snapshot, because making five million readers replay the whole match would be madness. The trade-off is the snapshot lags the log by milliseconds, which is invisible."
224	
225	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
226	> **Event log (Kafka)** — a durable, append-only, ordered stream of records that multiple consumers can read independently at their own pace. Here it holds the ordered balls so fan-out and analytics consume the same truth without stepping on each other.
227	> **Idempotency key** — a client-supplied unique tag on a write so that a retried request is applied only once. Here it stops a scorer's network retry from double-counting a six.
228	> **Materialized snapshot** — a precomputed current-state blob kept up to date as events arrive, so reads never recompute from raw events. Here it's the tiny score blob the hot path serves.
229	
230	**Diagram — write path:**
231	```mermaid
232	flowchart TD
233	    Scorer[Scorer app]:::client
234	    Scorer -->|"1 POST event"| Ingest[Ingest service]:::svc
235	    Ingest -->|"2 dedup check"| Dedup[(Idempotency store)]:::store
236	    Ingest -->|"3 append ordered"| Log[[Event log]]:::async
237	    Log -->|"4 consume"| Snap[Snapshot worker]:::svc
238	    Snap -->|"5 write current"| SnapStore[(Snapshot store)]:::store
239	    Log -.->|"6 also consume"| Analytics[Analytics pipeline]:::svc
240	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
241	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
242	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
243	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
244	```
245	
246	**Numbered steps:**
247	1. **Scorer POSTs the event.** Why ingest service: it owns auth + ordering. State: nothing yet. What could go wrong: flaky network → scorer retries → handled by step 2.
248	2. **Dedup check on the idempotency key.** Why: a retry must not double-apply. State: key recorded with TTL. Wrong: duplicate → we return the prior result, no double-count.
249	3. **Append to the event log with the per-match sequence.** Why: ordered, durable, replayable. State: the ball is now durable truth. Wrong: log unavailable → ingest returns 5xx, scorer retries (idempotent).
250	4. **Snapshot worker consumes the new event.** Why: it owns materialization. State: in-flight. Wrong: worker lag → snapshot trails; bounded and monitored.
251	5. **Worker writes the updated current snapshot.** Why: the hot path reads this. State: new score is live. Wrong: stale snapshot → bounded by worker lag (ms).
252	6. **Analytics pipeline independently consumes the same log.** Why: decoupled OLAP. State: async. Wrong: analytics down → live path unaffected (decoupled).
253	
254	> 💬 "The whole point of the log here: one ordered write, two independent consumers — the live fan-out and the analytics — never block each other."
255	
256	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The write is tiny but it's the trigger for everything. The scorer posts a ball with an idempotency key; we dedup, then append it to an ordered event log. A worker turns that log into a current snapshot — a tiny blob — so readers never replay history. The log also feeds analytics on a separate consumer, so a slow analytics job can never slow down the live score. The trade-off, a few milliseconds of snapshot lag, is invisible to a human watching cricket."
257	
258	### 6.2 — F2: Stream the live score to viewers (the huge read)
259	
260	This is the system. We must take that one snapshot update and deliver it to 50M screens in under 2s, while origin sees almost nothing.
261	
262	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off accepted |
263	|---|---|---|---|---|
264	| CDN edge | A global network of caches near users that holds the score blob | Collapses ~5M client req/s to ~50 origin req/s via short-TTL caching | Not origin-only — origin can't survive 5M/s | Score is up to TTL (~3–5s) stale on the poll path |
265	| Fan-out tier | A tree of edge boxes holding millions of WebSocket connections | Push delivers deltas in ~1s and is ~5x cheaper bytes than poll | Not have-everyone-poll — wastes bandwidth, stampedes on TTL | Must manage millions of stateful sockets |
266	| Pub/sub broker | A message bus that takes one publish and fans it to all subscribers | One write → many edge boxes without origin doing 50M sends | Not point-to-point from origin — origin would melt | Extra hop; a broker tier to operate |
267	
268	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "For viewers who poll, the CDN edge is the hero — a short TTL means the same blob is served to millions and only one request per edge per TTL ever reaches origin, collapsing five million a second down to about fifty. For viewers on our app, we push: a fan-out tier of edge boxes each holds about a hundred thousand persistent connections, and a pub/sub broker takes the single 'new ball' publish and fans it out to all of them, who each push a tiny delta down. I rejected origin-only and pure-poll because origin physically can't take five million a second and poll burns five times the bytes. The price is operating millions of stateful sockets and a broker tier."
269	
270	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
271	> **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — caches spread worldwide that serve content from near the user. Here it absorbs the poll firehose so origin barely sees traffic.
272	> **WebSocket / SSE** — a long-lived connection where the server pushes data without the client asking again. Here it delivers each ball in ~1s without polling.
273	> **Pub/sub (publish-subscribe)** — a broker where one publish is delivered to every subscriber. Here it turns one 'new ball' into a fan-out to all edge boxes.
274	> **Fan-out tree** — a multi-level structure (origin → brokers → edge boxes → clients) that amplifies one message to millions in stages so no single node sends 50M times.
275	> **TTL (time-to-live)** — how long a cached value is served before refetch. Here ~3–5s on the poll path balances freshness vs origin load.
276	
277	**Diagram — read fan-out (the hourglass):**
278	```mermaid
279	flowchart TD
280	    Viewer[Viewer app]:::client
281	    Viewer -->|"1 poll score"| CDN[CDN edge]:::ext
282	    Viewer -.->|"2 open socket"| Edge
283	    subgraph PushPath["Push path"]
284	        Broker[[Pub sub broker]]:::async
285	        Broker -->|"4 fan out"| Edge[Edge fan-out box]:::svc
286	        Edge -.->|"5 push delta"| Viewer
287	    end
288	    subgraph PollPath["Poll fallback"]
289	        CDN -->|"3 miss, one per TTL"| Origin[Origin read service]:::svc
290	    end
291	    SnapStore[(Snapshot store)]:::store
292	    Origin -->|"6 read snapshot"| SnapStore
293	    Broker -->|"7 reads from"| SnapStore
294	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
295	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
296	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
297	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
298	    classDef ext fill:#3a1a4c,stroke:#9b6bd9,color:#e6edf3
299	```
300	↳ reuses existing: Snapshot store (from §6.1)
301	
302	**Numbered steps:**
303	1. **Viewer polls the score** (fallback clients). Why CDN: it's near the user. State: served from edge cache. Wrong: edge miss → step 3.
304	2. **App viewer opens a WebSocket** to an edge fan-out box. Why: push beats poll on latency and bytes. State: one of ~50M sockets is held. Wrong: box full → routed to another box.
305	3. **On edge miss, ONE request per TTL hits origin.** Why: short TTL + stale-while-revalidate. State: edge refreshed. Wrong: stampede prevented by single-flight at edge.
306	4. **Broker fans the new ball out to all edge boxes.** Why: one publish → many. State: ~500 boxes notified. Wrong: a box lags → it catches up from the snapshot/ring buffer.
307	5. **Each edge box pushes a tiny delta** to its connections. Why: it owns those sockets. State: viewers updated in ~1s. Wrong: a socket is dead → client reconnects with `sinceSeq`.
308	6. **Origin reads the snapshot** on a miss. Why: snapshot is the cheap current blob. State: read. Wrong: snapshot store down → serve last-good from edge.
309	7. **Broker reads the latest snapshot** to attach to its publish. Why: deltas carry current state. State: read.
310	
311	> 💬 "One ball published once becomes fifty million pushes — but no single node ever sends fifty million times; the tree amplifies it in stages."
312	
313	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Here's the heart of it. When a ball lands, the snapshot updates and a pub/sub broker publishes it once. That one publish fans out through a tree — to a handful of regional brokers, then to about five hundred edge boxes, each holding a hundred thousand connections, and each box pushes a tiny three-hundred-byte delta down to its viewers. So one write becomes fifty million deliveries, but no node ever sends fifty million times — the tree amplifies in stages. For clients that can only poll, the CDN holds the blob with a few-second TTL, collapsing five million requests a second down to about fifty at origin. The trade-off is we now operate fifty million stateful sockets, but that's the price of sub-second freshness at this scale."
314	
315	### 6.3 — F3: Live stats & leaderboards (derived reads)
316	
317	A cumulative slice — it reuses the event log and adds derived aggregates.
318	
319	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off accepted |
320	|---|---|---|---|---|
321	| Stats worker | Consumes the event log, updates aggregates incrementally | Recompute-from-scratch on every event/read is wasteful; incremental is O(1) per event | Not on-read aggregation — scans every event per read | Aggregates lag the log slightly |
322	| Sorted-set store | An in-memory ranked structure (Redis sorted set) for leaderboards | Need top-N reads in O(log n) and live updates on each event | Not SQL ORDER BY over all matches — full scan per read | In-memory cost; rebuild on restart from log |
323	
324	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Stats and leaderboards are just another consumer of the same event log. A stats worker updates aggregates incrementally — when a six is hit, it bumps that one counter rather than rescanning the match. The cross-match leaderboard lives in a Redis sorted set, a ranked in-memory structure, so 'top scorers today' is an O(log n) read instead of a full scan across every match. The trade-off is the leaderboard lags by a beat and we rebuild it from the log on restart."
325	
326	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
327	> **Redis sorted set** — an in-memory collection that keeps members ranked by score, giving fast top-N and rank queries. Here it powers live leaderboards without scanning every match.
328	> **Incremental aggregation** — updating a running total per event rather than recomputing from all data. Here it keeps stats O(1) per ball instead of O(all-balls) per read.
329	
330	**Diagram:**
331	```mermaid
332	flowchart TD
333	    Log[[Event log]]:::async
334	    Log -->|"1 consume"| Stats[Stats worker]:::svc
335	    Stats -->|"2 incr aggregates"| Sorted[(Leaderboard sorted set)]:::store
336	    Viewer[Viewer app]:::client
337	    Viewer -->|"3 read top N"| Edge[Edge fan-out box]:::svc
338	    Edge -->|"4 read leaderboard"| Sorted
339	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
340	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
341	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
342	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
343	```
344	↳ reuses existing: Event log (§6.1), Edge fan-out box (§6.2)
345	
346	**Numbered steps:**
347	1. **Stats worker consumes the log.** Why: same ordered truth. State: in-flight. Wrong: worker lag → bounded staleness.
348	2. **Incrementally updates the sorted set.** Why: O(1) per event. State: rankings updated. Wrong: store down → rebuild from log.
349	3. **Viewer reads top-N** via the edge. Why: served like any read. State: read.
350	4. **Edge reads the leaderboard** from the sorted set. Why: O(log n). State: read.
351	
352	> 💬 "Leaderboards never scan every match — they're a sorted set updated one event at a time."
353	
354	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Stats reuse the same event log. A worker updates aggregates incrementally — bump one counter per ball — and the cross-match leaderboard lives in a Redis sorted set so top-N is a logarithmic read, not a scan across thousands of matches. It's eventually consistent by a second, which for 'most sixes today' nobody notices."

358	A different access pattern — heavier payload, deep history — so it gets its own store, not the hot path.

360	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off accepted |

365	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The full scorecard is a different beast — heavy payload, deep history, OLAP-style chart queries. So it reads from a separate analytics store fed by the same event log, never the hot live path. Once a match ends, the whole card is immutable and cacheable forever. I kept it off the live path so a heavy historical query can never slow the live score."

368	> **OLAP / columnar store** — a database laid out by column for fast scans and aggregations over lots of rows. Here it serves charts and ball-by-ball history without touching the live path.

438	## 7. Data Model & Storage
439	
440	### PART A — Entity table
441	
442	| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key bold) | Store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
443	|---|---|---|---|---|
444	| Match | **match_id (PK)**, teams, status, current_innings | Snapshot store (Redis) + metadata in SQL | match_id | Strong on write, eventual on viewer read |
445	| ScoreEvent | **(match_id, seq) (PK)**, type, payload, applied_at | Event log (Kafka) + cold copy in OLAP | match_id (partition) | Ordered, exactly-once apply |
446	| ScoreSnapshot | **match_id (PK)**, score, last_6_balls, last_seq | Snapshot store (Redis) | match_id | Eventual (lags log by ms) |
447	| Commentary | **(match_id, seq) (PK)**, text | Within event payload + OLAP | match_id | Eventual |
448	| Leaderboard | **board_id (PK)**, member→score | Redis sorted set | board_id | Eventual (rebuild from log) |
449	| MatchHistory | **(match_id, seq) (PK)**, full ball detail | OLAP (columnar) | match_id, date | Eventual; immutable after match |
450	
451	### PART B — Storage decision cards
452	
453	**🗄️ Redis (in-memory) — used for: ScoreSnapshot, Leaderboard, Idempotency store**
454	1. **What it is:** an in-memory key-value store that's extremely fast (microseconds) and has rich structures like sorted sets.
455	2. **Why we picked it:** the hot path serves the current snapshot to a firehose of readers; it must be in-memory, and leaderboards need a ranked structure — Redis sorted sets give top-N in O(log n).
456	3. **Considered & rejected:**
457	   - **Postgres for the snapshot** — disk round-trips per read are too slow for this fan-out, and it's not built for a ranked top-N read on every glance.
458	   - **Memcached** — a fine cache but no sorted sets, so leaderboards would need app-side logic.
459	   - **DynamoDB** — durable KV, but in-memory Redis is faster and cheaper for ephemeral hot data we can rebuild from the log.
460	4. **Trade-off:** it's ephemeral — a restart loses state, so we rebuild snapshots/leaderboards by replaying the event log.
461	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "The live score and leaderboards live in Redis because reads are everything and they must be in-memory. I rebuild it from the event log on restart, so losing it isn't losing truth — the log is truth."
462	
463	**🗄️ Event log / Kafka — used for: ScoreEvent (the source of truth)**
464	1. **What it is:** a durable, append-only, ordered stream that many consumers read independently at their own pace.
465	2. **Why we picked it:** it's the single ordered source of truth that decouples the live fan-out, the leaderboard worker, and analytics — each consumes the same balls without blocking the others, and we can replay to rebuild any derived state.
466	3. **Considered & rejected:**
467	   - **Direct DB inserts only** — loses replay and the multi-consumer decoupling; analytics queries would contend with the live path.
468	   - **A message queue (SQS/RabbitMQ)** — messages are consumed and gone; we need a *retained, replayable* ordered log, not a one-shot queue.
469	4. **Trade-off:** one more system to operate, but it's the backbone that makes everything else simple.
470	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Every ball goes into an ordered, durable log first. That's my source of truth — fan-out, leaderboards, and analytics all read it independently, and I can replay it to rebuild any cache I lose."
471	
472	**🗄️ CDN — used for: poll-fallback score blob & static scorecard**
473	1. **What it is:** a global network of caches near users that serves cached content without hitting origin.
474	2. **Why we picked it:** it collapses ~5M poll req/s into ~50 origin req/s via short-TTL caching — without it, origin dies.
475	3. **Considered & rejected:**
476	   - **Origin-only serving** — physically impossible at 5M req/s.
477	   - **App-tier cache only** — doesn't put bytes near the user; egress and latency both worse.
478	4. **Trade-off:** poll-path freshness is bounded by TTL (~3–5s).
479	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Polling clients hit a CDN with a few-second TTL, so millions of identical requests collapse into a handful at origin. The cost is the poll path can be a few seconds stale — fine for a fallback."
480	
481	**🗄️ OLAP / columnar store — used for: MatchHistory, full scorecard**
482	1. **What it is:** a database laid out by column for fast scans and aggregations over many rows.
483	2. **Why we picked it:** historical chart queries (wagon wheels, manhattans) scan lots of balls; columnar is built for that and keeps heavy queries off the live path.
484	3. **Considered & rejected:**
485	   - **Redis** — wrong shape and too expensive for cold deep history.
486	   - **Row-store SQL for analytics** — slow for big aggregate scans.
487	4. **Trade-off:** lags live by seconds — totally fine for a scorecard.
488	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Deep history and charts live in a columnar store fed by the log. It lags by seconds and never touches the live path, so a query for an old match can't slow the live score."
489	
490	### PART C — Per-operation consistency
491	
492	| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why right here |
493	|---|---|---|---|
494	| Apply a ball (write) | Event log | Ordered, exactly-once | The score must be correct and gap-free |
495	| Scorer reads own last ball | Snapshot/log | Read-from-primary | The one writer must always see their last submission |
496	| Viewer reads live score | Redis/edge | Eventual (~1–2s) | A human can't tell 1s; freshness > strict consistency |
497	| Leaderboard read | Redis sorted set | Eventual | "Top scorers today" tolerates a beat of lag |
498	| Scorecard / history | OLAP | Eventual, immutable post-match | History never changes once the match ends |
499	
500	### PART D — Data lifecycle (largest table: MatchHistory)
501	
502	MatchHistory grows with every ball of every match, forever — but at ~0.2 TB over five years (from §3) it's genuinely tiny, so retention is about *query performance*, not cost. We **time-partition by match date**; old seasons live in cheaper cold storage (S3/Glacier-class). Reclamation, if ever needed, is a **partition DROP** (instant, O(1)) — never row-by-row DELETE, which churns indexes and never cleanly reclaims space. Active-season partitions stay hot for fast scorecard reads; everything older is archived but still queryable for "this day in history."
503	
504	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The core stores are Redis for the live hot path — snapshots and leaderboards — and an ordered Kafka-style log as the actual source of truth, which is the key decision: I keep almost nothing authoritative in Redis, I rebuild it from the log. The CDN fronts the poll fallback, and a columnar store holds deep history for scorecards, fed by the same log so heavy queries never touch the live path. I considered putting the snapshot in Postgres but rejected it — disk round-trips can't feed this fan-out. The largest table is still only a couple hundred gigs, so I partition it by date for query speed, not because storage costs anything."
505

508	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
509	
510	> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the one number — 5M reads/s at origin if everyone polls — and reason forward: how do I make origin not see it? That's the spine of every dive here.
511	
512	### How do we serve 50M concurrent viewers without origin melting?
513	
514	**#### Bad: Everyone polls origin directly**
515	🗣️ **Plain words:** every phone asks our server "what's the score?" every few seconds.
516	**Approach:** clients `GET /score` on a timer; origin reads Redis and replies.
517	**Why people try this:** it's the simplest possible thing and works fine for a few thousand viewers.
518	**⚠️ What breaks:** at 50M viewers polling every 10s that's **~5M requests/second** at origin; at the last over (2s polling) it's **~25M/s**. No origin fleet or Redis serves that — the connection layer saturates and the whole match goes dark.
519	**🔁 Forces upgrade:** the instant concurrent viewers cross a few hundred thousand, origin QPS exceeds what any reasonable fleet handles.
520	
521	**#### Good: CDN-cached poll with short TTL + single-flight**
522	↩️ **What Bad got wrong:** Bad sent every identical request to origin; we cache the identical answer near the user.
523	🗣️ **Plain words:** put a few-second cached copy of the score in caches all over the world, so the millions of identical "what's the score" questions get answered locally.
524	**Approach:** the score blob is CDN-cached with `max-age=3-5s` and `stale-while-revalidate`. A **single-flight** mechanism (a database/edge guard so only ONE request refreshes a key while others wait or serve stale) means only one request per edge per TTL reaches origin.
525	**Why reasonable:** CDNs are built exactly for this read-amplification.
526	**⚠️ What breaks:** freshness is bounded by TTL (~3–5s) — fine normally, but in the last over people want sub-second; and a naive TTL expiry without single-flight causes a **thundering herd** (all edges miss at once → origin spike).
527	**🔁 Forces upgrade:** the demand for ~1s freshness in the last over, which a 5s TTL can't give.
528	
529	```mermaid
530	flowchart TD
531	    V1[Viewer 1]:::client --> Edge1[CDN edge]:::ext
532	    V2[Viewer 2]:::client --> Edge1
533	    V3[Viewer N]:::client --> Edge1
534	    Edge1 -->|"one miss per TTL, single flight"| Origin[Origin]:::svc
535	    Origin --> Redis[(Redis snapshot)]:::store
536	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
537	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
538	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
539	    classDef ext fill:#3a1a4c,stroke:#9b6bd9,color:#e6edf3
540	```
541	
542	**#### Great: Push via a multi-level fan-out tree**
543	↩️ **What Good got wrong:** polling is still pull — it wastes bytes (full blob every poll) and can't beat TTL latency; we flip to push so the server sends only when something changes.
544	🗣️ **Plain words:** instead of everyone asking, we hold an open line to every phone and the moment a ball is bowled we push a tiny note down all the lines at once.
545	**Approach:** clients hold a **WebSocket** to an edge fan-out box (~100K conns/box → ~500 boxes for 50M). A **pub/sub broker** takes the single "new ball" publish and fans it through a tree: origin → ~10 regional brokers → ~500 edge boxes → 50M clients. Each box pushes a ~300-byte **delta**. On reconnect the client sends `sinceSeq` and replays only the gap from a small ring buffer.
546	**Why this is the answer:** one write becomes 50M deliveries, but no node sends 50M times — the tree amplifies in stages; bytes drop ~5x vs poll; latency drops to ~1s.
547	
548	```mermaid
549	flowchart TD
550	    Origin[Origin publish]:::svc -->|"1 publish ball"| RB[Regional brokers]:::async
551	    RB -->|"2 fan out"| Edge1[Edge box 1<br/>100K conns]:::svc
552	    RB -->|"2 fan out"| Edge2[Edge box 500<br/>100K conns]:::svc
553	    Edge1 -.->|"3 push delta"| C1[50M viewers]:::client
554	    Edge2 -.->|"3 push delta"| C1
555	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
556	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
557	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
558	```
559	
560	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** 50M conns ÷ 100K per box = **~500 edge boxes**; one publish → 10 brokers → 500 boxes → 50M clients, so the **origin does ONE send per ball**. Poll would be 5M req/s; push makes origin's fan-out work O(brokers), not O(viewers).
561	
562	**✅ Failure matrix:**
563	| Scenario | What happens |
564	|---|---|
565	| Edge box dies | Its ~100K clients reconnect with `sinceSeq` → rebalanced to other boxes → replay gap, no missed ball |
566	| Broker partition | Affected region falls back to CDN poll; degraded freshness, never dark |
567	| Thundering reconnect | Jittered backoff + sticky routing spreads the herd; ring buffer serves catch-up |
568	| Snapshot stale | Delta carries `seq`; client detects gap and requests replay |
569	| Whole push tier down | Auto-fallback to CDN poll path; ~3–5s stale but live |
570	
571	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The naive answer — everyone polls — is five million requests a second at origin, which is impossible. The good fix is a CDN with a few-second TTL plus single-flight, which collapses that to about fifty a second; the catch is freshness is bounded by the TTL. The great answer is to push: hold a WebSocket to every viewer, about five hundred edge boxes at a hundred thousand connections each, and when a ball lands a pub/sub broker fans that one publish through a tree out to fifty million sockets as tiny deltas. One write, fifty million deliveries, but no node sends fifty million times — the tree amplifies in stages, latency drops to about a second, and bytes drop fivefold."
572	
573	> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just a bigger CDN TTL?":** "A longer TTL helps origin but hurts freshness — the whole point of a live-scores app is the last over feeling instant. Push decouples the two: origin load stays flat regardless of TTL, and freshness is sub-second."
574	
575	### How do we keep the ONE hot match from killing every other match?
576	
577	**#### Good: Shard fan-out by match_id**
578	🗣️ **Plain words:** give each match its own lane so a busy match can't clog the others.
579	**Approach:** consistent-hash connections and pub/sub topics by match_id; each match's traffic is isolated to its own set of edge boxes/topics.
580	**⚠️ What breaks:** the hot match's hash bucket lands on a fixed set of boxes — at 50M on one match, that bucket is 1000x the others; **the hot match overruns its own shard** even though others are fine.
581	**🔁 Forces upgrade:** a single match exceeding one shard's box capacity.
582	
583	**#### Great: Dedicated elastic pool + sub-sharding the hot match**
584	↩️ **What Good got wrong:** one match shouldn't be confined to one shard; we give the hot match its OWN elastic pool and split it across many boxes.
585	🗣️ **Plain words:** when one match goes viral, we spin it onto its own dedicated cluster of boxes and split its viewers across all of them.
586	**Approach:** detect the hot match (connection-count threshold), pre-warm a dedicated pool before a known marquee match, and **sub-shard** the match's 50M connections across N boxes (e.g. by viewer-id hash) all subscribed to the same pub/sub topic. The broker fans to all N. Small matches share a common pool.
587	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** 50M ÷ 100K/box = 500 dedicated boxes for the hot match alone; small matches together might need < 50 boxes. Isolation means the hot match's 500 boxes can't starve the small-match pool.
588	
589	```mermaid
590	flowchart TD
591	    Topic[[Hot match topic]]:::async -->|"fan out"| HotPool[Dedicated hot pool<br/>500 boxes]:::svc
592	    SmallTopic[[Small match topics]]:::async -->|"fan out"| SharedPool[Shared pool<br/>under 50 boxes]:::svc
593	    HotPool -.-> HotViewers[50M viewers]:::client
594	    SharedPool -.-> SmallViewers[other viewers]:::client
595	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
596	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
597	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
598	```
599	
600	**✅ Failure matrix:**
601	| Scenario | What happens |
602	|---|---|
603	| Hot match underestimated | Autoscaler adds boxes on connection-count threshold; brief queueing |
604	| Hot pool box dies | Its slice reconnects, rebalanced within the dedicated pool |
605	| Small match suddenly spikes | Promoted to its own pool when it crosses threshold |
606	
607	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The trap is treating all matches the same. If I just shard by match, the World Cup final lands on one shard that's a thousand times hotter than the rest. So the hot match gets its own dedicated, elastic pool — about five hundred boxes — and we sub-shard its fifty million connections across all of them, all subscribed to the one topic. Small matches share a tiny common pool. The blast radius is contained: the final can melt down without touching a domestic T20."
608	
609	> 🧠 **If they ask "how do you detect the hot match in time?":** "Two ways — scheduled pre-warming for known marquee fixtures, and a real-time connection-count threshold that promotes any match to its own pool when it crosses, say, half a million concurrent."
610	
611	### How do we keep the score correct — ordering & idempotency?
612	
613	**#### Good: Per-match sequence number**
614	🗣️ **Plain words:** number every ball so we know the order and can spot a missing one.
615	**Approach:** the single scorer assigns a monotonic `seq` per match; consumers apply in seq order, reject out-of-order, request replay on a gap.
616	**⚠️ What breaks:** the scorer's app retries on a flaky stadium network → the same ball POSTed twice → without dedup, a six counted twice.
617	
618	**#### Great: Sequence + idempotency key + single-writer**
619	↩️ **What Good got wrong:** ordering alone doesn't stop a *retry duplicate* of the same seq; add an idempotency key.
620	🗣️ **Plain words:** each submission carries a unique tag, so a retry is recognized and applied only once.
621	**Approach:** there is exactly **one authoritative writer** per match (no write conflicts), each event has `(match_id, seq)` plus an `idempotencyKey`; the ingest service dedups on the key with a TTL, and the log enforces ordering. A retry returns the original result.
622	**✅ Failure matrix:**
623	| Scenario | What happens |
624	|---|---|
625	| Retry same ball | Idempotency key matches → return original, no double-count |
626	| Out-of-order arrival | Consumer buffers, applies in seq order |
627	| Gap (missed seq) | Consumer requests replay from log |
628	| Two scorers (shouldn't happen) | Second is rejected — single-writer enforced by match lease |
629	
630	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Correctness is easy here because there's exactly one writer per match — no conflict resolution needed. Every ball has a per-match sequence number for ordering and an idempotency key so the scorer's network retry doesn't double-count. Consumers apply in order and request a replay if they see a gap. The single-writer assumption is doing a lot of work — it's why this is a fan-out problem, not a consensus problem."
631	
632	> 🧠 **If they ask "what if you needed multiple scorers?":** "Then I'd need a write lease or consensus to pick the authoritative one — but that's a different, much stricter design, and I'd flag the scope change."
633	
634	### How do we handle back-pressure when the fan-out can't keep up?
635	
636	**#### Great: Bounded queues + shed reads first, drop stale deltas**
637	🗣️ **Plain words:** if the system gets overwhelmed, we slow down or skip the least-important stuff and protect the core score.
638	**Approach:** every fan-out queue is **bounded** (never unbounded — that's a latent outage). The load-shedding order under overload: drop **commentary and stats deltas first**, then coalesce score deltas (send only the latest, skip intermediate balls), and only as a last resort shed connections to the poll fallback. Trigger signals: pub/sub consumer lag and edge-box queue depth. Because deltas are idempotent and carry `seq`, a dropped intermediate delta self-heals — the next delta or a `sinceSeq` resync brings the client current.
639	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** if an edge box's outbound queue exceeds, say, 1s of depth, it coalesces — at ~1 event/15s, coalescing to "latest score" loses nothing a human perceives.
640	
641	```mermaid
642	flowchart TD
643	    Broker[[Broker]]:::async -->|"deltas"| Q[Bounded edge queue]:::async
644	    Q -->|"under load coalesce"| Push[Push latest only]:::svc
645	    Q -.->|"overflow shed commentary"| Drop[Drop non-critical]:::svc
646	    Push -.-> Viewer[Viewer]:::client
647	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
648	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
649	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
650	```
651	
652	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Under overload I never let a queue grow unbounded — that's how you turn a spike into an outage. Each edge queue is bounded; when it backs up, I coalesce score deltas down to just the latest and shed commentary and stats first, protecting the bare score. Because deltas carry a sequence number and are idempotent, dropping an intermediate one self-heals on the next push. The trigger is consumer lag or queue depth crossing a threshold."
653	
654	> 🧠 **If they ask "won't coalescing lose balls?":** "Only intermediate frames — the latest score is always correct, and at one ball every fifteen seconds coalescing during a millisecond spike is invisible. The scorecard, fed by the log, still has every ball."
655

658	## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost
659	
660	### 9A — Availability targets
661	
662	- **Read path: 4 nines (99.99%)** = **~52 minutes of downtime per *year* total** — and ideally zero during a final. Mechanisms: global CDN, the push fan-out has no single origin in the hot path, **multi-AZ** (multiple data centres in one region so one losing power doesn't take us down) for origin/Redis, and a poll fallback that survives even if push is down.
663	- **Write path: 3 nines (99.9%)** = ~8.7 hours/year — looser because the write is tiny and a brief ingest blip just delays a ball; the scorer retries idempotently and the log catches up.
664	
665	### 9B — Per-dependency failure table
666	
667	| Component | What breaks | How it degrades (what USER SEES) | Recovery |
668	|---|---|---|---|
669	| CDN edge | Poll fallback unavailable | Push clients unaffected; poll clients retry other PoPs | CDN auto-reroutes PoPs |
670	| Pub/sub broker | Push fan-out stalls for a region | That region falls back to CDN poll — score is ~3–5s stale, never dark | Broker failover; clients reconnect |
671	| Edge fan-out box | ~100K sockets drop | Those viewers see a brief "reconnecting," then catch up via `sinceSeq` | Reconnect + rebalance, replay gap |
672	| Redis snapshot | Hot reads fail | Origin serves last-good from log replay; brief staleness | Rebuild snapshot from event log |
673	| Event log | Writes can't append | Scorer POST returns 5xx, retries; score freezes briefly | Log failover; idempotent retry catches up |
674	| OLAP store | Scorecard/history unavailable | Live score fine; detailed card shows "loading" | Rebuild from log; isolated from live path |
675	
676	### 9C — RPO / RTO
677	
678	> **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we'd lose if it crashed right now. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until we're back up.
679	
680	| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
681	|---|---|---|---|
682	| Event log (truth) | ~0 (replicated before ack) | ~minutes | Multi-broker replication; nothing acked is lost |
683	| Snapshot / Redis | tolerant (rebuildable) | ~seconds | Replay from log — it's a cache, not truth |
684	| Leaderboard | tolerant (rebuildable) | ~seconds | Replay from log |
685	| OLAP history | minutes of lag OK | ~minutes | Re-derive from log; immutable post-match |
686	
687	The key insight: **only the event log needs RPO≈0**; everything else is rebuildable from it, so DR is "protect the log, replay the rest."
688	
689	### 9D — Cost breakdown
690	
691	- **CDN/push egress — the #1 cost.** Every byte pushed to a screen is billed. At peak, ~7 TB/hour of deltas; a marquee month of peak play is the dominant line item (~$14K just for the hot peak hours, far more across a full tournament).
692	- **Fan-out compute — #2.** ~500 edge boxes holding 50M sockets during the hot match; mostly idle-connection cost.
693	- **Redis memory — #3, but small.** Hot snapshots + leaderboards fit in tens of GB.
694	- **Biggest optimization:** push **deltas, not full pages** — ~5x fewer bytes than poll (computed in §3). Secondary: aggressive gzip/binary framing on deltas and coalescing under load.
695	- **Unit economics:** **~$0.015 per 1M deliveries** — the metric to watch as we scale.
696	
697	> 🎙️ **Script:** "When something fails, the user almost never goes dark — that's the design. If the push tier hiccups, they silently fall back to the few-seconds-stale poll path. If an edge box dies, they see a half-second 'reconnecting' and catch up from where they left off. The only thing I guard with RPO-zero is the event log, because everything else — snapshots, leaderboards, history — I can rebuild by replaying it. And the bill is bandwidth, full stop, which is exactly why I push tiny diffs instead of whole pages."
698

701	## 10. Trade-off Ledger
702	
703	**🔀 Decision: Push (WebSocket fan-out) vs Poll (CDN)**
704	1. **Chose & why:** push, for ~1s freshness and ~5x cheaper bytes.
705	2. **Gave up:** simplicity — we now operate 50M stateful sockets and a fan-out tree.
706	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Like keeping a phone line open to everyone instead of having everyone call us every few seconds — fewer wasted calls, but we manage a lot of open lines."
707	4. **When this reverses:** if we must support dumb/embed clients that can't hold a socket, poll-via-CDN becomes primary and we accept ~3–5s freshness.
708	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I push because it's faster and cheaper per byte; if you need to support arbitrary browsers, I flip to CDN polling and trade a couple seconds of freshness for dead-simple clients."
709	
710	**🔀 Decision: Materialized snapshot vs replay-on-read**
711	1. **Chose & why:** materialize the current score once per ball.
712	2. **Gave up:** a few ms of snapshot lag behind the log.
713	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Keep a scoreboard on the wall instead of re-reading the whole logbook every time someone looks."
714	4. **When this reverses:** never at this scale — replay-on-read at 5M reads/s is a non-starter.
715	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I materialize because making millions of readers replay the match is insane; the cost is milliseconds of lag nobody perceives."
716	
717	**🔀 Decision: Dedicated hot-match pool vs uniform sharding**
718	1. **Chose & why:** isolate the hot match so it can't starve others.
719	2. **Gave up:** capacity efficiency — the dedicated pool is over-provisioned for the final.
720	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Give the superstar match its own stadium so the crowd doesn't crush the little games next door."
721	4. **When this reverses:** if load is genuinely uniform across many small matches, uniform sharding is simpler and cheaper.
722	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I dedicate a pool to the hot match for blast-radius isolation; if there's no single hot match, I drop it and shard uniformly."
723	
724	**CAP/PACELC:** the live read path is **AP** — under partition we favor availability (serve slightly stale via poll fallback) over consistency. Under normal operation (PACELC's "else") we favor **latency over consistency** — ~1s eventual freshness beats blocking for a strongly-consistent read. The event log apply is the one **CP** corner: ordered, exactly-once, single-writer.
725	
726	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'd defend hardest: push over poll, because freshness and bytes both win, reversible if clients can't hold sockets. Materializing the snapshot — non-negotiable at this scale. And dedicating a pool to the hot match for isolation, which I'd drop if there's no single hot match. The whole read path is AP and latency-first — I'd rather show a one-second-old score to everyone than block; the only strongly-consistent corner is appending the ball to the log."
727

730	## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers
731	
732	**❓ "Two clients see slightly different scores for a moment — is that a bug?"**
733	The mechanism: viewers are served from different edge boxes/PoPs that receive a delta within ~1s of each other, so for a few hundred ms one screen can be one ball ahead. This is intentional eventual consistency on the read path.
734	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Yeah, for half a second one phone can be a ball ahead of another — and that's fine, nobody's betting on it through us."
735	💬 "It's eventual within ~1–2s by design — strict cross-viewer consistency would cost latency we don't need."
736	
737	**❓ "What happens in the last over when everyone refreshes at once?"**
738	The mechanism: with push there's no refresh — viewers already hold a socket, so a last-over spike just means more *deltas pushed*, not more requests; and the dedicated hot pool is pre-warmed. Polling clients hit the CDN with stale-while-revalidate so origin sees ~50 req/s regardless.
739	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Push clients don't refresh — the line's already open, we just push the ball. Pollers hit a cache, so origin barely notices."
740	💬 "Push converts a refresh storm into a fan-out, which we already sized for."
741	
742	**❓ "An edge box holding 100K connections crashes — what do those users see?"**
743	The mechanism: the 100K sockets drop; clients auto-reconnect with `sinceSeq`, get rebalanced to a healthy box, and replay the missed balls from a ring buffer — no ball is lost.
744	🗣️ **In plain words:** "They see 'reconnecting' for a blink, then catch up exactly where they left off."
745	💬 "Reconnect with a resume token replays the gap — losing a box never loses a ball."
746	
747	**❓ "Can the same ball be counted twice?"**
748	The mechanism: each event has an idempotency key; ingest dedups on it with a TTL, and the log enforces per-match sequence ordering, so a scorer's retry returns the original result.
749	🗣️ **In plain words:** "No — every submission has a unique tag, so a retry is recognized and ignored."
750	💬 "Idempotency key plus single-writer per match — duplicates and conflicts both gone."
751	
752	**❓ "How do you stop a fake event from being injected?"**
753	The mechanism: the write endpoint requires a JWT proving the caller is the assigned scorer for THAT match (object-level authz, not just authn); viewers can't write at all, and events can be signed.
754	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Only the one authorized scorer for that specific match can post a ball — we check it's them and it's their match."
755	💬 "Object-level authz on the write — 'is this the scorer for THIS match,' not just 'is this a valid user.'"
756	
757	**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?"**
758	The mechanism: CDN/push egress dominates — bytes to screens. Biggest cut: push deltas not full pages (~5x), plus binary framing and coalescing under load. Storage and DB are rounding errors (~0.2 TB).
759	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Bandwidth is the bill. Sending tiny diffs instead of whole pages cuts it about fivefold."
760	💬 "Egress, full stop — deltas over pages is the single biggest lever, computed at ~5x."
761	
762	**❓ "Why a fan-out tree instead of origin pushing to everyone?"**
763	The mechanism: origin pushing to 50M clients is 50M sends from one place — it melts. A tree (origin → ~10 brokers → ~500 boxes → 50M) means each node fans to a manageable degree; origin does ONE publish.
764	🗣️ **In plain words:** "One server can't push to fifty million directly — we amplify the message in stages, like a phone tree."
765	💬 "The tree turns one publish into 50M deliveries with no node doing 50M sends."
766	
767	**❓ "What if the pub/sub broker for a region goes down?"**
768	The mechanism: that region's push stalls; clients fall back to the CDN poll path (~3–5s stale) and reconnect when the broker recovers; other regions are unaffected.
769	🗣️ **In plain words:** "That region gets the slightly-slower polling version for a bit, never a blank screen."
770	💬 "Broker loss degrades to poll, not to dark — graceful by region."
771	
772	**❓ "How would you add live video later?"**
773	The mechanism: video is a separate, much higher-bandwidth CDN/HLS pipeline; it hangs off the same match_id but shares nothing on the hot path — our score deltas can even sync overlays onto the video.
774	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Video is its own beast — a separate streaming pipeline keyed by the same match; our scores just sync the overlay."
775	💬 "Add it as a sibling system on match_id, not bolted onto the score fan-out."
776	
777	**❓ "How do you rate-limit abusive pollers or socket spammers?"**
778	The mechanism: a **token-bucket** limiter (a fixed refill rate per client) at the edge; state lives in the edge/Redis (itself a coordinator we keep simple), and over-limit clients get **HTTP 429 + Retry-After**. Socket churn is capped per IP.
779	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Each client gets a budget of requests; over it, we tell them to back off and wait."
780	💬 "Token bucket at the edge, 429 with Retry-After — and a per-IP cap on reconnect storms."
781	
782	**❓ "How do you observe this — how do you know it's healthy during a final?"**
783	The mechanism: golden signals — latency (delivery lag), traffic (concurrent conns), errors (reconnect rate), saturation (queue depth/consumer lag) — with **burn-rate alerts** on the SLO, and a **trace-id** propagated gateway → log → broker → edge so one ball is traceable end to end across the async hop.
784	🗣️ **In plain words:** "We watch how fast balls reach screens, how many people are on, error rates, and how backed up the queues are — and we can trace a single ball's whole journey."
785	💬 "Golden signals plus end-to-end trace-id on the ball; burn-rate alerts catch trouble before users do."
786	
787	**❓ "Can a viewer miss a ball entirely?"**
788	The mechanism: deltas carry `seq`; the client detects a gap and replays from the ring buffer / `sinceSeq`. Coalescing under load only drops *intermediate* frames — the latest score is always delivered.
789	🗣️ **In plain words:** "If your phone misses a frame, it notices the gap and re-asks for it — you always land on the right score."
790	💬 "Sequence-numbered deltas self-heal gaps; the latest score is never dropped."
791	
792	**❓ "What if the event log itself is lost?"**
793	The mechanism: the log is replicated across brokers and acked only after replication (RPO≈0), so it's the one thing we protect hardest; everything downstream (snapshot, leaderboard, OLAP) is rebuilt by replaying it.
794	🗣️ **In plain words:** "The log is the one thing I never lose — and if I lose everything else, I just replay it to rebuild."
795	💬 "Protect the log at RPO-zero; the rest is derivable, so DR is replay."
796	
797	**❓ "Why not store the live score in Postgres?"**
798	The mechanism: Postgres disk round-trips can't feed a 5M-read/s fan-out and aren't built for ranked leaderboard reads; Redis serves in-memory in microseconds and the log is the durable truth.
799	🗣️ **In plain words:** "A disk database is too slow for this read firehose; the fast in-memory store plus a durable log gives me both speed and safety."
800	💬 "Redis for speed, log for truth — Postgres can't feed this read rate."
801	
802	> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "It's a live cricket scores platform that pushes one ball out to tens of millions of screens in under two seconds. The design splits into two halves. The read half is the whole system: a CDN absorbs any polling, collapsing five million requests a second to about fifty at origin, and a push fan-out tree — five hundred edge boxes holding fifty million WebSockets, fed by a pub/sub broker — delivers tiny deltas so one write becomes fifty million deliveries without any node sending fifty million times. The write half is almost trivial: about one event every fifteen seconds, made correct by a per-match sequence number and an idempotency key, with exactly one authoritative scorer per match so there are no write conflicts. The two halves connect through an ordered, durable event log, which is the single source of truth that the live fan-out, the leaderboards, and the analytics all consume independently. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: this is a read-fan-out problem, not a database problem — so spend the entire budget on getting one tiny write out to fifty million screens cheaply."
